questionText: Consider the claim that the product of any two consecutive integers is even. Express this claim symbolically using a universal quantifier, explain why it is a single universally quantified statement rather than an implication with a separate hypothesis, and then prove it.

steps.description: Translate the claim into symbols using a universal quantifier.
steps.explanation: Two consecutive integers can be written $n$ and $n+1$, so their product is $n(n+1)$. The words 'for any' become the symbol $\forall$, and the domain $\mathbb{Z}$ records that $n$ ranges over all integers.

steps.description: Explain the logical shape of the statement.
steps.explanation: The only restriction on $n$ is that it is an integer, which the quantifier already carries as its domain. There is no separate hypothesis to assume, so this is a plain universally quantified statement, not an implication.

steps.description: State the key intuition behind the proof.
steps.workingLatex: n,\ n+1:\ \text{one is even, one is odd}
steps.explanation: Consecutive integers alternate in parity, so exactly one of $n$ and $n+1$ is even. Multiplying anything by an even number gives an even result, which is why the product must be even.

steps.description: Set up a proof by cases on the parity of n.
steps.explanation: Every integer is either even or odd, so splitting into these two cases covers all possibilities for $n$. Proving the claim in each case therefore proves it for all integers.
If $n$ itself is even we write $n=2k$; the factor of $2$ passes straight into the product. Since $k(n+1)$ is an integer, the product is twice an integer and hence even.

steps.description: Case 2: n is odd, so n+1 is even.
steps.explanation: If $n$ is odd, then its successor $n+1$ is even, and we can factor a $2$ out of $n+1$ instead. This is the case where the even number is the second of the pair.

steps.description: Complete Case 2 by factoring out the 2.
steps.explanation: The even factor $2(k+1)$ makes the whole product a multiple of $2$. As $n(k+1)$ is an integer, the product is again even.
In both of the only two possibilities for $n$, the product $n(n+1)$ came out even. Since these cases exhaust the integers, no value of $n$ escapes the conclusion.

steps.description: State the proved universally quantified statement.
steps.explanation: Because the argument held for an arbitrary integer split into its two possible parities, it holds for every integer. This confirms the original $\forall$ statement, with the domain $\mathbb{Z}$ as its only condition.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The claim is $\forall n \in \mathbb{Z},\ n(n+1)\text{ is even}$ — a universally quantified statement whose only condition is the domain $\mathbb{Z}$, not a separate hypothesis. Proof by cases: if $n=2k$ then $n(n+1)=2[k(n+1)]$; if $n=2k+1$ then $n+1=2(k+1)$ so $n(n+1)=2[n(k+1)]$. Either way the product is even.
